episodes.title: The method
episodes.intro_text: You are Juror 12 at Westhaven Crown Court. The Crown alleges the defendant obtained jewellery and other valuables from several women by false pretences, posing as a wealthy gentleman with a written list of clothing and a worthless cheque. This episode opens with the first two complainants.

episodes.title: Many accounts
episodes.intro_text: Two more complainants describe the same pattern: a titled stranger, a written list, a sealed cheque, and jewellery taken for sizing. A police inspector then explains how the women were asked to pick out the accused.

blocks.text: Mrs Elling testified she was a widow in Regent's Park that December. About six o'clock one Monday evening she stood in Bond Street in mourning when a well-dressed gentleman spoke to her, said she must be a very young widow, and asked how her husband had died. After talking for about a quarter of an hour she gave him her address though she rarely received gentlemen. He said he was dining out and would write. He did not give his name.

Next morning she received a letter on Grand Hotel notepaper asking her to expect him between one and two. He arrived by cab, removed his coat, and sat by the fire. He described a house in St John's Wood and offered her work as his housekeeper, saying the previous lady was being sent away without money though she kept jewellery he had given her. He promised five pounds a week to begin, rising to ten if she suited him, and spoke of Lincolnshire estates and grapes to be sent next day.

He said she must leave off mourning and wrote a list of dresses and jewellery she was to order, without her suggesting items. He named shops and handed her a cheque for fifteen guineas to pay a ten-pound deposit, having two cheques already written in his pocket. He sealed the cheque in an envelope addressed to a bank at Belgrave Mansions. When she asked if it could be changed nearer home he refused, saying a gentleman in his position could not have cheques changed casually.

He asked to see her rings, took her wedding ring and another for sizing, and promised their return about five o'clock by a one-armed commissionaire from the Grand Hotel, together with jewellery he would buy. The rings were worth about five or six pounds. After he left she found a brooch missing from her dressing table. She showed the cheque to her brother and, on his advice, never presented it and did not go to the police at the time.

In January, after reading newspaper reports of a man charged with similar frauds, she went to the police court without contacting the police first. She saw the accused in the dock, recognised him at once from his back, and said she would know him among a thousand. She was quite sure he was the same man.

source.locator: NBT p. 120, examination; S-03 Morning Advertiser cross-check

blocks.text: Under cross-examination she admitted reading two or three newspaper accounts, including in the Standard and the People, before attending court. She knew a man was in custody for conduct like her own complaint. She was angry about the wedding ring. She had stood in the street with him ten to fifteen minutes near a lighted shop window and was calm at both meetings.

He was middle-aged with light hair tinged grey, very well dressed, with a gold watch and heavy chain. She could not say whether he faced her in the street. She identified him by his back at the police court and had never identified anyone before. One ring bore the motto Love and Friendship inside; the brooch was a mounted five-shilling piece enamelled with a date.

She attended the police court four times and heard one other lady give evidence. Nothing was peculiar about his dress except the watch and chain. She saw him write the list freely and heard him say the departing housekeeper had jewellery worth eight thousand pounds.

Re-examined, she said he wore a blue overcoat with a velvet collar in Bond Street and a black frock coat with fawn spats when he called. The shop was well lighted and she saw his face clearly during a visit of one to one and a half hours. She thought him English though he might have had a slight foreign accent.

Mrs Garner lived in St John's Wood. On a July afternoon in St James's Street she was with a lady and a small boy when the accused spoke to her, gave the name Wilton, and said he would call. She did not know him. He wrote her address in a pocket book and called the next afternoon about four o'clock, staying three-quarters of an hour.

When he called she said neither she nor anyone she knew recognised the name. He claimed it was an excuse to speak to her and said he was Lord Wilton. He described an empty house in St John's Wood and suggested she move there. When she said she was happy where she was, he offered better dresses and jewellery and wrote a list on paper she supplied.

He arranged to return the following Monday and gave her a cheque for thirty-five pounds in an envelope addressed to a bank in St James's Street, saying she should pay a deposit. He asked for a ring to measure her finger and she gave him a small diamond ring and a dented gold bracelet he offered to repair. He said a one-armed commissionaire would return them within an hour.

She also showed him a marquise ring in a case and left the room twice for matches and a photograph. After he left she missed the marquise ring, worth about fifteen pounds though she had first valued all he took at that sum. She went to St James's Street but could find no Union Bank there.

On 9 July she gave the police a description. On 16 December she was sent for, viewed seven or eight men at the station, and recognised the accused after asking that he remove his hat. She was with him in her rooms for three-quarters of an hour and now said he was the man.

Under cross-examination she agreed she was influenced by his removing his hat when picking him out. She described him as between fifty and sixty, rather short, stout, with a dark complexion and a short brown moustache turning grey. She had never seen him before in St James's Street though she later learned he lived nearby.

The lady with her in the street walked on and did not attend court. When he called he wore a frock coat, white waistcoat lining, white spats, and a heavy gold chain. He wrote normally but signed the cheque holding the pen between two fingers. She was sent for soon after his arrest and had not read a description of him beforehand.

Another witness spoke to her at the police court about what she had lost. Among the men shown she noticed the accused at once but would not swear until his hat came off. Another man had a grey moustache but was too tall with a ruddy face. In the street he wore a low round hat and she thought a brownish coat; he dressed differently when he called.
Re-examined, she said the property could be easily identified. She valued the loss higher once she knew the marquise ring's true worth. Her name and address were not found among the accused's papers.

source.locator: NBT pp. 136, re-examination (inferred from examination close)

blocks.text: Mrs Holt testified that in February she lived in Upper Baker Street. On a Saturday about one o'clock she was passing through Ludgate Hill with her sister when a well-dressed man, who she believed to be the accused, asked if she was waiting for an omnibus. After brief conversation he asked her address and permission to call the next day, which she gave.

He called the next day about two, announced as Mr Wilton, and said he was the Earl of Wilton. He sat with her for three-quarters of an hour, wrote a list of dresses, and spoke of a house in Abbey Road, St John's Wood with conservatories, servants, and good wine. He proposed she dine there on the Tuesday to see whether she liked the place.

She was to order at Redfern's, a riding habit at Cobb's in Baker Street, and boots at Norman's in Victoria Street. He tore a cheque for forty pounds from a cheque-book, wrote and signed it with his hand shielding the paper, and put it in an envelope addressed to a bank at Belgravia Mansions. He asked for her rings to size replacements and took her wedding ring and a plain foreign gold ring, promising return by a commissionaire from Streeter's.

Next morning she found no London and South-Western Bank at Belgravia Mansions, opened the envelope, and later sent the cheque to the Balham branch, which refused payment and kept it. The commissionaire never came and the rings were not returned. She made no complaint to the police at the time.

On the second of January the police fetched her to Westminster Police Court, where she picked the accused out from about a dozen men. She touched him with her umbrella. She thought at the time of their talks that he was not English, though he claimed an English title. She had no doubt he was the man.

source.locator: NBT pp. 124, examination; Cd. 2315 p. 77 cross-check

blocks.text: Under cross-examination she agreed the police came to her after seeing her name on the cheque. She had been with him only about two minutes in the street; her sister saw him there but did not attend court. She read about the case in the papers after being subpoenaed and knew a man was in custody.

The accused looked slightly thinner now and his moustache was not waxed as before, but she remained pretty sure of him. He was remarkably well dressed with spats and a black frock coat, a black satin tie with a large pearl pin, and something white inside his waistcoat. She could not say whether he had a watch and chain.

Five or six women attended to identify him; they went in one by one and she went first. She hesitated only because she disliked the task. She did not touch anyone else. When he wrote the dress list he wrote freely but indistinctly, holding his pen backwards.

Re-examined, she said she was the first to identify him and was surprised later to see Miss Taylor among the witnesses. They had not spoken about the case beforehand and neither helped the other to point him out. Apart from being thinner and the moustache, he looked much as he had in February and at the police court.

Mrs Irwin testified she was a widow living in a flat in Shaftesbury Avenue. On the sixth of March about one fifteen she was walking in Piccadilly with her little daughter when the accused asked if she was Lady somebody; she said she was not. He asked where she lived and whether she knew Lord Aberdeen, and she thought from that she had met him before. He kept a handkerchief to the left side of his face for about three minutes.

He called the next afternoon about three forty, again partly concealing his face while her sister admitted him. He said he was Lord Winton de Willoughby, had just returned from Canada, and had stayed with the Earl of Aberdeen. He offered a house in St John's Wood with twelve servants, a carriage, and a wine cellar, and dictated a list of clothes she should order, including items from Redfern's.

He wrote a cheque for one hundred and twenty pounds, sealed it in an envelope, and promised jewellery from Streeter's. She gave her wedding ring for sizing and lent a thick gold curb bracelet, her sister's bracelet, an ostrich-feather fan, a pair of elephant tusks, and a hand-painted porcelain photograph. He said a one-armed commissionaire would return the ring.

She left the room to fetch tea. Two or three hours after he left she missed tiger claws and teeth of an animal mounted in silver with her monogram. She valued what he took at about one hundred and eighty pounds. Next day the Balham bank kept the cheque and it was dishonoured. Two days later she reported the matter at Vine Street and gave a description.

She did not see him again until late December, when police brought her to Westminster Police Court. She viewed thirteen men in the yard and at once recognised the accused. When she heard him speak she was doubtful, because in her flat he had a Yankee twang but in court he used a foreign accent she thought Swiss. His hands, hair, and feet seemed the same.

Under cross-examination she agreed she might have met him in India, where she had lived eleven years, but did not recognise him from that. In Piccadilly he wore a black overcoat with a velvet collar and spats. When he called it was very dark and he kept the handkerchief to his face while her sister let him in.

She made the articles into a parcel for him; none was found in his possession though she could identify them. No other man shown with him was like him. She read the case in the papers but had spoken to the police before that. She described him as about fifty-three, with no whiskers and the same moustache as now.

He seemed to write freely though she did not watch closely because his back was towards her. He appeared to have a gold watch and chain. She gave police a full list of her property. He looked just the same now as then and fidgeted at her flat, refusing the tea prepared for him.

Re-examined, she said at the police court he wore an overcoat like the man's and fawn-coloured spats when he called. At the station she heard him speak a foreign language to an interpreter; it was not French and might have been Norwegian. She felt he wanted to leave as quickly as possible.

Inspector Vale testified that he took charge of the case after the third remand in January, succeeding a local inspector who had handled earlier hearings. Before that he had not been responsible for the accused's clothing being left at his lodgings, which he would not have allowed.

He conducted the remaining identifications at Westminster Police Court after taking charge, including Mrs Elling and three other complainants whose cases were still outstanding. He chose men put up with him as far as possible resembling the accused, gave the women no hint, and let the accused place himself where he liked among fourteen or sixteen others.

Several women identified the accused under his supervision. He noticed they seemed to feel sympathy for him on account of his age. Two women had failed to identify at an earlier December hearing and five in all had failed, while others identified but would give no names or addresses.

He did not regard identification in the dock alone as sufficient unless supported by other identifications. Suitable members of the public could not be compelled to stand in a line; the police had to take whom they could find in the street and adjoining shops.

He searched the accused's box at the Covent Garden Hotel and found white and brown spats, several white waistcoats, underwear, an opera hat, a wedding ring, photographs of ladies and views of Norway and Sweden, and a large quantity of business correspondence.

Under cross-examination he understood the accused had stayed at the hotel until the previous September and later at the Buckingham Hotel in the Strand. He did not go to Victoria Street and took charge only after the third of January. The waistcoats produced were typical of those found.

He had inquired about a Mrs Gardiner, who had been convicted of passing bad cheques and pretending to be a person of distinction, with a warrant out for assault later withdrawn by her landlady. Despite every effort he could not find her to subpoena as a witness.

Re-examined, he repeated that he was not in charge until the third remand and would not have left the prisoner's clothes at his lodgings. He had made every effort to trace Mrs Gardiner for the defence.

exhibits.title: Clothing list (Mrs Elling)
exhibits.text: Handwritten list headed dresses and mourning wear to be ordered, including bonnets (spelled bonets in the original), silk gowns, gloves, and mourning trim. The accused specified named shops including a court dressmaker.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: Shows the method of building trust through an elaborate wardrobe order.
exhibits.defence_claim: The list proves nothing about identity or intent to defraud.

source.locator: Cd. 2315 facsimile; NBT p. 119 list Q

exhibits.title: Clothing list (Mrs Garner)
exhibits.text: Second list of dresses and accessories written for Mrs Garner on paper she supplied, with items to be ordered before a further appointment on the following Monday.
exhibits.prosecution_claim: Repeats the same fraudulent pattern used against Mrs Elling.
exhibits.defence_claim: A list alone does not show the accused wrote it or meant to cheat.

source.locator: NBT pp. 135–136, list in Daisy Grant examination
